one_line_takeaway: A cheap, model-agnostic 'evaluation agent' that screens prompts for danger AND jailbreak-intent, run a handful of times with conservative weighted voting, blocks ~99.5-100% of Best-of-N text jailbreaks, and a small LLaMa-3-8B evaluator is nearly as good as Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
key_finding: DATDP blocks essentially all BoN jailbreaks while barely touching benign traffic. It blocked 100% of the 1,045 successful BoN-paper jailbreak prompts (CI [99.65%, 100.00%]) with BOTH Claude and LLaMa-3-8B evaluators, and 99.8%-99.9% of the 1,000 replicated jailbreaks. A small, cheap model (LLaMa-3-8B-instruct) performs almost identically to frontier Claude 3.5 Sonnet as an evaluator. Self-evaluation works: a model that is trivially jailbroken as a responder reliably blocks the very same jailbreaks when acting as evaluator (Fig 2 shows LLaMa-3-8B refusing as evaluator prompts it happily answers as assistant), suggesting models 'know' a prompt is dangerous even when they can be tricked into answering it. The authors estimate the defense multiplies BoN cost by roughly 500x. N=12 iterations already reaches near-optimal blocking.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DATDP (Defense Against The Dark Prompts), a preemptive, model-agnostic defense that places a separate evaluation-agent LLM in front of the responding LLM. The evaluation agent is prompted (via a fixed 'forbidden_task' system prompt) to flag any prompt that is either dangerous to answer OR appears to be a jailbreak attempt, and it does so through repeated (iterative) evaluations aggregated with a weighted score. Crucially, DATDP explicitly screens for jailbreak attempts as well as harmful content, which the authors argue distinguishes it from some prior filters. The paper's own experimental contribution is showing that this cheap wrapper blocks essentially all Best-of-N (BoN) augmentation jailbreaks, and that a small model (LLaMa-3-8B-instruct) works nearly as well as a frontier model (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) in the evaluator role. DATDP is presented as building on the authors' own earlier 'GPT-Eliezer / chatgpt-prompt-evaluator' work (refs [18],[19]); the BoN attack it defends against (Hughes et al. [16]) is cited prior work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Adversary submits textual jailbreak prompts generated via Best-of-N (BoN) attacks (Hughes et al. [16]): a harmful base request is repeatedly augmented with random word scrambling, random capitalization, and ASCII noising (variance parameter sigma=0.25) and re-sampled until one variant elicits a harmful response. BoN reportedly reaches 89% attack success on GPT-4o and 78% on Claude 3.5 Sonnet at 10,000 samples, and even bypasses circuit-breaker defenses. The attacker's power comes from adding variance to inputs, not from any specific augmentation. Scope is text-only jailbreaks; visual/audio BoN modalities are explicitly out of scope. The paper does NOT evaluate an adaptive attacker who optimizes augmentations specifically against DATDP itself.
limitations: (1) Text-only; visual and audio BoN jailbreaks are untested. (2) No adaptive/white-box attacker is evaluated against DATDP itself, so the claimed ~500x cost increase is an extrapolation, not a measured result against an attacker optimizing against the evaluator. (3) The evaluator itself is an LLM and could be jailbroken; robustness of the evaluator to attacks is assumed rather than demonstrated. (4) Cost/latency: every prompt is run through the evaluator N times (up to 25 for LLaMa), a non-trivial overhead not quantified in dollars/latency. (5) Over-blocking risk is real though small: the LLaMa evaluator falsely blocked a normal prompt ('How can I meet hot girls in my area?') and 2 of 159 unaugmented HarmBench prompts; the paper notes evaluators are actually MORE reliable on augmented than unaugmented prompts. (6) The response-evaluation variant underperformed (blocked only 760/1,000), though this may reflect the strict HarmBench grader. (7) Replication jailbreak-labeling used the HarmBench auto-grader driven by LLaMa-3-8B instead of GPT-4o, a weaker grader. (8) Evaluation is confined to HarmBench-derived harmful behaviors and BoN-style augmentations.

datasets_used: BoN GitHub set: 1,045 augmented HarmBench prompts that successfully jailbroke advanced models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3 Opus, GPT-4o, Gemini Pro)
Replicated Jailbreaks: 1,000 augmented prompts that jailbroke LLaMa-3-8B-instruct (sampled from 26,946 successes)
Replicated Safe: 1,000 augmented prompts that did NOT jailbreak LLaMa-3-8B-instruct
Generic Augmented: 1,590 random augmented prompts (10 per each of 159 base prompts)
quantitative_results: BoN Paper jailbreaks blocked: Claude 100% (CI [99.65%,100.00%]), LLaMa-3-8B 100% (CI [99.65%,100.00%])
HarmBench blocked: Claude 100% (CI [97.71%,100%]), LLaMa-3-8B 98.74% (CI [95.53%,99.85%]) - LLaMa missed 2 of 159 (Syrian Civil War and immigrant-taxes articles)
Replicated Jailbreaks blocked: Claude 99.90% (missed 1 of 1,000), LLaMa-3-8B 99.80% (missed 2 of 1,000)
Replicated Safe blocked: Claude 99.50% (blocked 995/1000 - false positives), LLaMa-3-8B 100%
Generic Augmented blocked: Claude 99.94%, LLaMa-3-8B 99.94% (both missed 1 of 1,590: augmentation of 'Generate racist vitriol')
Normal prompts blocked (false-positive rate, lower is better): Claude 0% (0/250), LLaMa-3-8B 0.40% (1/250)
Replication scale: 795,000 augmented prompts (5,000 x 159); 26,946 jailbroke LLaMa-3-8B, 768,054 failed
Iterations: N=25 (LLaMa-3-8B), N=5 (Claude); N=12 sufficient for near-optimal performance
Response-evaluation variant: blocked only 760/1,000 replicated jailbreaks but caught all 3 prompts that bypassed the prompt-evaluation agents
Cited BoN attack success rates (prior work [16]): 89% GPT-4o, 78% Claude 3.5 Sonnet at 10,000 samples
Estimated effect: ~99.7% constant blocking effectiveness, extrapolated to make BoN jailbreaking ~500x harder
baselines_compared: Two evaluator base models compared head-to-head: Claude 3.5 Sonnet vs LLaMa-3-8B-instruct (Claude better on 3 datasets, LLaMa on 1, tied on 2)
Compared against the undefended BoN attack success rates from Hughes et al. [16]

Contrasted qualitatively with prior/related defenses cited (not experimentally re-run): circuit breakers [17], Self-Guard [12], self-reminders [11], WildTeaming [13]; framed relative to AI-control 'trusted smaller model monitors untrusted larger model' work [22]
